modsem 0.1.3

    Customizing Interaction Terms Using lavaan functions The LMS and QML approaches methods Quadratic Effects

Quadratic Effects

Source: vignettes/quadratic.Rmd
quadratic.Rmd
library(modsem)

In essence quadratic effects are just a special case of interaction effects – where a variable has an interaction effect with itself. Thus, all of the modsem methods can be used to estimate quadratic effects as well.

Here you can see a very simple example using the LMS-approach.

library(modsem)
m1 <- '
# Outer Model
X =~ x1 + x2 + x3
Y =~ y1 + y2 + y3
Z =~ z1 + z2 + z3

# Inner model
Y ~ X + Z + Z:X + X:X
'

est1Lms <- modsem(m1, data = oneInt, method = "lms")
summary(est1Lms)
#> Estimating null model
#> EM: Iteration =     1, LogLik =   -17831.87, Change = -17831.875
#> EM: Iteration =     2, LogLik =   -17831.87, Change =      0.000
#> Calculating Hessian
#> 
#> ----Model Summary------------------------------------------------------
#> 
#> Number of iterations: 31 
#> Final loglikelihood: -14806.26 
#> 
#> Comparative fit to H0 (no interaction effect)
#>  Loglikelihood change = 3025.61
#>  D(2) = -6051.23, p = <2e-16
#> 
#> R-squared:
#>    Y = 0.596 
#> R-squared Null-Model (H0):
#>    Y = 0.395 
#> R-squared Change:
#>    Y = 0.201 
#> 
#> ----Estimates----------------------------------------------------------
#> 
#> Loadings:
#>   X: 
#>    variable    est std.error t.value p.value ci.lower ci.upper
#>          x1  1.000        NA      NA      NA       NA       NA
#>          x2  0.807     0.012  67.459  <2e-16    0.783    0.830
#>          x3  0.917     0.013  71.874  <2e-16    0.892    0.942 
#>   Z: 
#>    variable    est std.error t.value p.value ci.lower ci.upper
#>          z1  1.000        NA      NA      NA       NA       NA
#>          z2  0.810     0.012  65.118  <2e-16    0.786    0.835
#>          z3  0.881     0.013  67.667  <2e-16    0.856    0.907 
#>   Y: 
#>    variable    est std.error t.value p.value ci.lower ci.upper
#>          y1  1.000        NA      NA      NA       NA       NA
#>          y2  0.798     0.007 107.573  <2e-16    0.784    0.813
#>          y3  0.899     0.008 112.611  <2e-16    0.884    0.915 
#> 
#> Regressions: 
#>   Y: 
#>    variable    est std.error t.value p.value ci.lower ci.upper
#>           X  0.687     0.030  22.636  <2e-16    0.628    0.747
#>           Z  0.606     0.025  24.024  <2e-16    0.556    0.655
#>         X:X -0.009     0.020  -0.436   0.663   -0.047    0.030
#>         X:Z  0.727     0.028  26.230  <2e-16    0.672    0.781 
#> 
#> Intercepts: 
#>    variable    est std.error t.value p.value ci.lower ci.upper
#>          x1  1.065     0.009 115.033  <2e-16    1.047    1.083
#>          x2  1.249     0.009 137.332  <2e-16    1.231    1.267
#>          x3  0.958     0.009 103.619  <2e-16    0.940    0.976
#>          z1  1.028     0.024  42.912  <2e-16    0.981    1.075
#>          z2  1.219     0.020  60.807  <2e-16    1.180    1.258
#>          z3  0.930     0.021  43.374  <2e-16    0.888    0.972
#>          y1  1.094     0.033  33.355  <2e-16    1.030    1.158
#>          y2  1.266     0.027  47.448  <2e-16    1.214    1.318
#>          y3  1.005     0.030  33.740  <2e-16    0.947    1.064 
#> 
#> Variances:
#>    variable    est std.error t.value p.value ci.lower ci.upper
#>          x1  0.171     0.005  31.623  <2e-16    0.160    0.182
#>          x2  0.165     0.005  31.623  <2e-16    0.155    0.175
#>          x3  0.171     0.005  31.623  <2e-16    0.160    0.181
#>          z1  0.167     0.009  18.560  <2e-16    0.149    0.184
#>          z2  0.160     0.007  22.724  <2e-16    0.146    0.173
#>          z3  0.158     0.008  20.833  <2e-16    0.143    0.173
#>          y1  0.160     0.009  18.051  <2e-16    0.142    0.177
#>          y2  0.154     0.007  22.711  <2e-16    0.141    0.168
#>          y3  0.164     0.008  20.727  <2e-16    0.148    0.179
#>           X  0.912     0.009 101.460  <2e-16    0.895    0.930
#>           Z  0.184     0.022   8.274  <2e-16    0.140    0.227
#>           Z  1.015     0.018  55.185  <2e-16    0.979    1.051
#>           Y  0.990     0.036  27.226  <2e-16    0.919    1.062

In this example we have a simple model with two quadratic effects and one interaction effect, using the QML- and double centering approach, using the data from a subset of the PISA 2006 data.

m2 <- '
ENJ =~ enjoy1 + enjoy2 + enjoy3 + enjoy4 + enjoy5
CAREER =~ career1 + career2 + career3 + career4
SC =~ academic1 + academic2 + academic3 + academic4 + academic5 + academic6
CAREER ~ ENJ + SC + ENJ:ENJ + SC:SC + ENJ:SC
'

est2Dblcent <- modsem(m2, data = jordan)
est2Qml <- modsem(m2, data = jordan, method = "qml")
summary(est2Qml)
#> Estimating null model
#> Starting M-step
#> Calculating Hessian
#> Warning in calcSE(final$hessian): SE's for some coefficients could not be
#> computed.
#> Warning in calcD(estH0, object): D is positive, this indicates that the null
#> model is better than the alternative model. Returning '-D' instead of 'D'
#> 
#> ----Model Summary------------------------------------------------------
#> 
#> Number of iterations: 11 
#> Final loglikelihood: -110522.6 
#> 
#> Comparative fit to H0 (no interaction effect)
#>  Loglikelihood change = -1.33
#>  D(3) = -2.66, p = 0.447
#> 
#> R-squared:
#>    CAREER = 0.520 
#> R-squared Null-Model (H0):
#>    CAREER = 0.510 
#> R-squared Change:
#>    CAREER = 0.010 
#> 
#> ----Estimates----------------------------------------------------------
#> 
#> Loadings:
#>   ENJ: 
#>     variable    est std.error t.value p.value ci.lower ci.upper
#>       enjoy1  1.000        NA      NA      NA       NA       NA
#>       enjoy2  1.012     0.020  49.918  <2e-16    0.972    1.051
#>       enjoy3  0.905     0.021  43.259  <2e-16    0.864    0.946
#>       enjoy4  1.011     0.021  47.621  <2e-16    0.969    1.053
#>       enjoy5  1.059     0.021  49.700  <2e-16    1.017    1.101 
#>   SC: 
#>     variable    est std.error t.value p.value ci.lower ci.upper
#>    academic1  1.000        NA      NA      NA       NA       NA
#>    academic2  1.102     0.028  39.182  <2e-16    1.047    1.158
#>    academic3  1.232     0.029  42.005  <2e-16    1.175    1.289
#>    academic4  1.250     0.030  42.128  <2e-16    1.192    1.308
#>    academic5  1.113     0.029  38.888  <2e-16    1.057    1.169
#>    academic6  1.196     0.029  40.631  <2e-16    1.139    1.254 
#>   CAREER: 
#>     variable    est std.error t.value p.value ci.lower ci.upper
#>      career1  1.000        NA      NA      NA       NA       NA
#>      career2  1.036     0.016  65.458  <2e-16    1.005    1.067
#>      career3  0.948     0.016  58.015  <2e-16    0.916    0.980
#>      career4  0.814     0.017  48.412  <2e-16    0.781    0.847 
#> 
#> Regressions: 
#>   CAREER: 
#>     variable    est std.error t.value p.value ci.lower ci.upper
#>          ENJ  0.548     0.021  26.619  <2e-16    0.508    0.588
#>           SC  0.458     0.024  19.482  <2e-16    0.412    0.505
#>      ENJ:ENJ  0.064     0.023   2.814  0.0049    0.019    0.109
#>       ENJ:SC -0.114     0.048  -2.388  0.0170   -0.207   -0.020
#>        SC:SC  0.033     0.035   0.941  0.3468   -0.036    0.101 
#> 
#> Intercepts: 
#>     variable    est std.error t.value p.value ci.lower ci.upper
#>       enjoy1  0.002     0.013   0.139  0.8896   -0.023    0.027
#>       enjoy2  0.002     0.013   0.170  0.8651   -0.023    0.027
#>       enjoy3  0.001     0.013   0.086  0.9315   -0.024    0.026
#>       enjoy4  0.002     0.013   0.152  0.8792   -0.023    0.027
#>       enjoy5  0.002     0.013   0.183  0.8550   -0.023    0.027
#>    academic1  0.000     0.012   0.016  0.9876   -0.024    0.025
#>    academic2  0.001     0.013   0.051  0.9590   -0.024    0.025
#>    academic3  0.000     0.012   0.021  0.9835   -0.024    0.025
#>    academic4  0.001     0.012   0.042  0.9663   -0.024    0.025
#>    academic5  0.000     0.013   0.011  0.9911   -0.025    0.025
#>    academic6  0.001     0.013   0.113  0.9099   -0.023    0.026
#>      career1 -0.016     0.017  -0.942  0.3463   -0.048    0.017
#>      career2 -0.020     0.017  -1.157  0.2471   -0.053    0.014
#>      career3 -0.016     0.016  -0.959  0.3373   -0.048    0.016
#>      career4 -0.014     0.015  -0.885  0.3762   -0.044    0.017 
#> 
#> Variances:
#>     variable    est std.error t.value p.value ci.lower ci.upper
#>       enjoy1  0.489     0.011  44.369  <2e-16    0.468    0.511
#>       enjoy2  0.490     0.011  44.406  <2e-16    0.468    0.511
#>       enjoy3  0.595     0.012  48.226  <2e-16    0.571    0.619
#>       enjoy4  0.487     0.011  44.527  <2e-16    0.465    0.508
#>       enjoy5  0.442     0.010  42.428  <2e-16    0.421    0.462
#>    academic1  0.645     0.013  49.801  <2e-16    0.619    0.670
#>    academic2  0.566     0.012  47.888  <2e-16    0.542    0.589
#>    academic3  0.474     0.011  44.317  <2e-16    0.453    0.495
#>    academic4  0.456     0.010  43.581  <2e-16    0.435    0.476
#>    academic5  0.565     0.012  47.712  <2e-16    0.541    0.588
#>    academic6  0.502     0.011  45.454  <2e-16    0.480    0.523
#>      career1  0.372     0.009  40.348  <2e-16    0.354    0.390
#>      career2  0.327     0.009  37.077  <2e-16    0.310    0.345
#>      career3  0.436     0.010  43.358  <2e-16    0.416    0.456
#>      career4  0.578     0.012  48.380  <2e-16    0.554    0.601
#>          ENJ  0.490     0.017  29.004  <2e-16    0.457    0.523
#>           SC  0.216     0.008  25.503  <2e-16    0.200    0.233
#>           SC  0.338     0.014  23.437  <2e-16    0.310    0.367
#>       CAREER  0.301     0.010  29.681  <2e-16    0.281    0.321

Note: The other approaches work as well, but might be quite slow depending on the number of interaction effects (particularly for the LMS- and constrained approach).

Developed by Kjell Solem Slupphaug.
